ArchitectureNotes.objective: Provide a generic safe administrative package for ocserv that favors constrained execution, explicit confirmation for dangerous actions, and strong auditability over broad convenience.
ArchitectureNotes.non-goal: Do not expand MVP into route orchestration, LAN publishing, firewall automation, or general-purpose infrastructure control.
ArchitectureNotes.phase-0-anchor: Before implementation waves begin, freeze the access vocabulary so user means VPN identity, group means ocserv policy group, network-group wording means access rules or routes, and the operator surface stays limited to the approved public MCP tool vocabulary.
ArchitectureNotes.risk-1: Unsafe coupling between orchestration and system mutation could bypass confirmation or write-scope safeguards.
ArchitectureNotes.risk-2: Reload and policy-file changes need verification-first sequencing to avoid service disruption or unrelated config corruption.

contract.purpose: Emit structured audit and operational records for every approved or rejected administrative action.

notes.note-1: Design fields and redaction rules before wiring higher-risk modules.

contract.purpose: Enforce allowlists, confirmation rules, dangerous-action guards, and operator rate limits before any system mutation occurs.

export-guardAction.PURPOSE: Evaluate whether an action may proceed

export-confirmDestructiveAction.PURPOSE: Track and verify explicit confirmation for destructive actions

export-checkRateLimit.PURPOSE: Throttle repeated operator requests at the entry boundary

notes.note-1: This module gates destructive flows and should land before adapters that can mutate system state.

contract.purpose: Track pending destructive actions, explicit confirmations, expiry, and replay-safe resolution state.

export-createPendingConfirmation.PURPOSE: Create a pending confirmation record for a destructive action

export-resolvePendingConfirmation.PURPOSE: Resolve a confirmation as confirmed, cancelled, expired, or replayed

notes.note-1: Keep confirmation state deterministic so orchestrator retries and repeated MCP tool calls cannot replay destructive actions.

contract.purpose: Wrap occtl, deterministic managed config files, validation, reload flows, post-change health checks, and rollback-safe activation in a constrained system adapter.

export-inventoryConfig.PURPOSE: Inventory managed ocserv config, auth, and user-group mapping surfaces

export-preflightMutation.PURPOSE: Validate mutation preconditions and declare the deterministic write scope

export-applyManagedMutation.PURPOSE: Apply mutate -> validate -> reload -> verify with rollback on failure

export-runOcctl.PURPOSE: Run occtl commands and normalize structured output

export-safeReload.PURPOSE: Validate config and reload ocserv safely

export-healthCheck.PURPOSE: Run the approved ocserv health check command

export-rollbackLastChange.PURPOSE: Restore the last config-affecting managed-file change set

notes.note-1: Prefer narrow subprocess wrappers that are easy to fake in tests.
notes.note-2: The adapter owns the deterministic managed file set for main config, group policy files, auth store, and user-group mapping.

contract.purpose: Own VPN identity creation, disablement, removal, and existence checks against the chosen ocserv auth backend.

export-createUser.PURPOSE: Create a VPN identity through the approved auth backend path

export-disableUser.PURPOSE: Disable a VPN identity without silently deleting it

export-removeUser.PURPOSE: Delete a VPN identity through the approved lifecycle path

notes.note-1: This module makes user lifecycle ownership explicit instead of spreading create, disable, and remove semantics across the API and adapter layers.
notes.note-2: Lifecycle mutations must return deterministic planned-file, changed-file, activation, and verification evidence to the API layer.

contract.purpose: Apply intended policy-group assignments and policy rendering updates without touching unrelated config.

export-assignGroup.PURPOSE: Assign a user to an ocserv policy group

notes.note-1: Write scope and file-diff assertions are key evidence for this module.
notes.note-2: Group assignment must operate only through the adapter-owned managed file set and emit validate -> reload -> verify evidence.

contract.purpose: Provide real session listing backed by occtl output.

notes.note-1: Use parser tests against representative occtl outputs before wiring tool-facing flows.
notes.note-2: Session termination is approved for the public MCP tool surface and must stay confirmation-gated through the shared backend flow.

M-OCSERV-ADMIN-API.NAME: ocserv_admin_api

contract.purpose: Expose constrained localhost HTTP endpoints for approved user, session, group, and reload actions that only accept authenticated loopback clients.

export-executeApprovedAction.PURPOSE: Run one approved admin action through constrained handlers

M-OCSERV-ADMIN-API.depends: M-SAFETY-CONTROLS, M-USER-LIFECYCLE, M-POLICY-GROUP-MANAGER, M-SESSION-MANAGER, M-OCSERV-ADAPTER, M-AUDIT-LOG

notes.note-1: Keep the API surface narrow, action-whitelisted, bound to localhost, and protected by a shared MCP-client-to-backend bearer token.

contract.purpose: Provide a strict client boundary for approved backend actions and staged confirmation flows without direct administrative execution.

export-planAction.PURPOSE: Validate an approved action request and normalize it for backend execution

notes.note-1: MCP clients must call only the localhost backend and must not execute ocserv administrative work directly.
notes.note-2: The package boundary accepts only schema-valid tool invocations and does not perform free-text command interpretation.

contract.purpose: Expose the approved ocserv admin actions as a strict local MCP stdio server for MCP-capable agents.

export-OcservAdminMcpServer.PURPOSE: Serve initialize, tools/list, and tools/call over stdio

export-buildServerFromEnv.PURPOSE: Build the MCP server from the localhost backend configuration

notes.note-1: The MCP server must expose only the approved MVP actions and never provide shell or direct config-edit tools.

DF-ADD-USER.NAME: Add user with policy group
DF-ADD-USER.TRIGGER: Authorized operator requests user creation through an approved MCP client
DF-ADD-USER.step-1: The client submits an approved create-user tool call with schema-valid inputs.
DF-ADD-USER.step-2: The MCP server or direct backend client routes the approved action to the localhost admin API.
DF-ADD-USER.step-3: Admin API invokes user-lifecycle, policy-group, and ocserv adapter modules to create the user, assign the intended group, sync managed templates, validate config, request a safe reload, and verify the resulting state.
DF-ADD-USER.step-4: When the passwd-file backend is active, the operator receives a one-time provisioning credential in the successful create-user response.
DF-ADD-USER.step-5: Audit log records intent, deterministic file scope, activation evidence, and target identity without exposing credentials.
DF-ADD-USER.evidence: Verified user visibility after reload, scoped config changes, deterministic planned-file and changed-file evidence, secure one-time credential handoff for the passwd-file backend, and stable audit/log markers across the flow.

DF-REMOVE-USER.NAME: Remove or disable user safely
DF-REMOVE-USER.TRIGGER: Authorized operator requests destructive identity change through an approved MCP client
DF-REMOVE-USER.step-1: The client submits a destructive approved action request for one intended VPN identity.
DF-REMOVE-USER.step-2: Confirmation state records a pending destructive action and gates confirm or cancel transitions.
DF-REMOVE-USER.step-3: Safety controls require explicit confirmation before execution.
DF-REMOVE-USER.step-4: Admin API executes the approved destructive action through user-lifecycle and constrained adapter modules, blocks active-user removal without force, then records the outcome.
DF-REMOVE-USER.evidence: Confirmation state, active-session force checks, correct target identity, and audit entries proving the destructive action did not run silently.

DF-SHOW-SESSIONS.NAME: Inspect active sessions
DF-SHOW-SESSIONS.TRIGGER: Authorized operator requests active session visibility through an approved MCP client
DF-SHOW-SESSIONS.step-1: The MCP server or direct backend client routes the approved list-sessions action to the localhost admin API.
DF-SHOW-SESSIONS.step-2: Session manager queries real session state from occtl through the ocserv adapter.
DF-SHOW-SESSIONS.step-3: Structured session records are normalized for the operator-facing response.
DF-SHOW-SESSIONS.step-4: Audit context records the inspection request and outcome when routed through controlled operator surfaces.
DF-SHOW-SESSIONS.evidence: Parsed occtl output and stable structured session-state results.

DF-DISCONNECT-SESSION.NAME: Disconnect active session safely
DF-DISCONNECT-SESSION.TRIGGER: Authorized operator requests termination of an active VPN session through an approved MCP client
DF-DISCONNECT-SESSION.step-1: The client submits the disconnect request as a destructive action and routes it through the shared confirmation flow.
DF-DISCONNECT-SESSION.step-2: Confirmation state records a pending destructive action and requires confirm or cancel before execution.
DF-DISCONNECT-SESSION.step-3: Session manager calls the adapter-owned occtl disconnect surface for the requested VPN identity.
DF-DISCONNECT-SESSION.step-4: Audit log records both the confirmation boundary and the disconnect outcome.
DF-DISCONNECT-SESSION.evidence: Confirmation token lifecycle, structured disconnect command result, and audit entries proving the disconnect did not run silently.

DF-ASSIGN-GROUP.NAME: Assign policy group without collateral edits
DF-ASSIGN-GROUP.TRIGGER: Authorized operator changes a user group through an approved MCP client
DF-ASSIGN-GROUP.step-1: The client treats the change as destructive and requires explicit confirmation before execution.
DF-ASSIGN-GROUP.step-2: Group manager computes the intended policy-file change set for one user and one group.
DF-ASSIGN-GROUP.step-3: Adapter writes only approved targets and validates the resulting config.
DF-ASSIGN-GROUP.step-4: Safe reload runs if validation passes and the adapter verifies the requested final state.
DF-ASSIGN-GROUP.evidence: Confirmation token lifecycle, file-diff evidence shows only intended config changed, unrelated policies remained intact, and verification confirms the requested user-group assignment.

DF-SAFE-RELOAD.NAME: Validate then reload with rollback-oriented behavior
DF-SAFE-RELOAD.TRIGGER: A staged config change requires activation
DF-SAFE-RELOAD.step-1: Adapter runs config validation before any reload attempt.
DF-SAFE-RELOAD.step-2: If validation fails, execution stops and the system records a failure audit event.
DF-SAFE-RELOAD.step-3: If reload or verify fails, managed files roll back to the pre-mutation snapshot and a clear failure record is emitted.
DF-SAFE-RELOAD.evidence: Validation status, reload status, verify status, rollback evidence, and audit evidence for stop-on-failure behavior.

Phase-0.name: Access model lock
Phase-0.goal: Remove ambiguity in the meaning of user, group, network-group wording, and the minimum public MCP tool surface before module implementation begins.
Phase-0.step-1: Lock the MVP domain model around User, PolicyGroup, Session, and CommandAuditEntry as the canonical access vocabulary.
Phase-0.step-2: Freeze the public MCP tool surface to list_users, list_sessions, disconnect_session, create_user, disable_user, delete_user, assign_group, reload_service, rollback_last_change, and confirm_action.
Phase-0.evidence: requirements.xml, knowledge-graph.xml, and verification-plan.xml record the same access semantics and tool surface.

Phase-1.name: Safety foundation
Phase-1.goal: Establish audit, guard, and adapter primitives before any operator-facing flow can mutate ocserv state.
Phase-1.step-1: Implement structured audit logging and redaction-aware event schema.
Phase-1.step-2: Implement allowlists, confirmation primitives, and dangerous-action guards.
Phase-1.step-3: Implement pending confirmation tracking, expiry, and replay-safe resolution.
Phase-1.step-4: Implement constrained occtl, validation, and reload wrappers with safe failure behavior.

Phase-2.name: Administrative core flows
Phase-2.goal: Make user, group, and session operations deterministic and testable on top of the safe adapter layer.
Phase-2.step-1: Implement explicit create, disable, and remove lifecycle operations for VPN identities.
Phase-2.step-2: Implement policy-group assignment and scoped rendering logic.
Phase-2.step-3: Implement active session listing and approved disconnect behavior.
Phase-2.step-4: Expose the constrained admin API over approved actions.

Phase-3.name: Operator surfaces
Phase-3.goal: Wire controlled MCP interaction surfaces without weakening the safety and audit model.
Phase-3.step-1: Implement approved-action validation and confirmation-aware orchestration.
Phase-3.step-2: Expose the approved action vocabulary as strict MCP tools over local stdio transport.

ExecutionPolicy.worker-owns: Only approved module source files and module-local tests inside the scoped execution packet.
